questions.prompt: What kind of charge was it?

Automatic non-conviction sealing for not-guilty, no-bill, or no-probable-cause outcomes (§ 100C)
Court-requested sealing for dismissal or nolle prosequi (§ 100C)
Non-time-based expungement for false identity, error, fraud, or decriminalized conduct (§ 100K)

questions.prompt: Was this an adult criminal case, juvenile delinquency/youthful offender case, or are you not sure?
How did the case end: guilty/conviction, CWOF, dismissal, nolle prosequi, not guilty, no bill, no probable cause, or still pending?
Was it a misdemeanor or felony, and when did you finish jail, probation, parole, or any sentence?
Were all the charges from before your 21st birthday, and do you have no more than two records total?
Was the record created because of identity theft, false identification, court/police/witness error, fraud, or because the conduct is no longer a crime?
How old were you when this happened?
Have you paid off everything the court charged?
Was this arrest a mistake — wrong person, identity theft, or an error?
Which court or agency handled the case?
What does the record say you were charged with?

pathways.label: Adult conviction sealing under M.G.L. c. 276, § 100A
pathways.summary: Adult conviction sealing is governed mainly by M.G.L. c. 276, 100A. Record type Waiting period Clock rule Misdemeanor 3 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration or custody, must be at least 3 years before request Felony 7 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration or custody, must be at least 7 years before request Sex offense 15 years or longer 15 years after disposition/supervision/incarceration, or as long as registration duty continues, whichever is longer
pathways.sourceRef: section:43-68; overview:7-26; section:7-26; section:27-42; corpus-unit:7; corpus-unit:10; corpus-unit:27; corpus-unit:19
ruleClauses: Adult conviction sealing is governed mainly by M.G.L. c. 276, 100A.
Record type Waiting period Clock rule
Misdemeanor 3 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration or custody, must be at least 3 years before request
Felony 7 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration or custody, must be at least 7 years before request
Sex offense 15 years or longer 15 years after disposition/supervision/incarceration, or as long as registration duty continues, whichever is longer
Offense no longer a crime Immediate / forthwith Unless the same conduct remains criminal under a different designation

Section 100A requires no Massachusetts guilty finding during the applicable 3- or 7-year period, except minor motor-vehicle offenses punishable by a fine of not more than $50. It also requires the petitioner to state that they have no disqualifying out-of-state, federal, or U.S. possession convictions/imprisonment during the same lookback period. (
"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/section100a")
Adult sealing disqualifiers / special handling
Firearms convictions under M.G.L. c. 140 121-131H Generally not sealable under 100A
Public-corruption / obstruction-type convictions under c. 268 or c. 268A Generally not sealable under 100A, except resisting arrest
Sex offenses 15-year wait or registration-duty period, whichever is longer
Level 2 or Level 3 sex offender classification Not eligible to seal sex offenses
209A abuse-prevention order violation / 258E harassment-prevention order violation Treat as felony for sealing timing
Subsequent not guilty, dismissal, nolle prosequi, no bill Does not interrupt the waiting period
Old felony now classified as misdemeanor Treat as misdemeanor
Unknown felony/misdemeanor level Treat as misdemeanor
These rules come directly from 100As eligibility and exception language. (
Marijuana-only offense Marijuana expungement check Petition for Expungement of Marijuana Offenses; usually tied to 100K/decriminalized conduct logic

Massachusetts defines expungement as the permanent erasure or destruction of a record so it is no longer accessible to or maintained by the court, criminal justice agencies, or other government agencies. That is stronger than sealing. (
"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100E")

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100B?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

pathways.label: Time-based expungement under §§ 100F–100J
pathways.summary: Time-based expungement is available only in limited cases. It runs through 100F, 100G, 100H, 100I, and 100J. Record type Statute Basic route Juvenile delinquent / youthful offender adjudication 100F Petition Commissioner of Probation Adult conviction 100G Petition Commissioner of Probation Adult or juvenile record without conviction/adjudication 100H Petition Commissioner of Probation
pathways.sourceRef: section:97-137; overview:7-26; section:138-169; section:7-26; section:230-287; section:180-191; section:192-202; section:218-229
ruleClauses: Time-based expungement is available only in limited cases. It runs through 100F, 100G, 100H, 100I, and 100J.
Record type Statute Basic route
Juvenile delinquent / youthful offender adjudication 100F Petition Commissioner of Probation
Adult conviction 100G Petition Commissioner of Probation
Adult or juvenile record without conviction/adjudication 100H Petition Commissioner of Probation

For conviction expungement, 100G allows a petitioner with not more than 2 records of conviction to petition the Commissioner of Probation; multiple offenses from the same incident count as one offense for this purpose. The Commissioner certifies eligibility under 100I and 100J, the district attorney gets notice, and the court may grant or deny based on the best interests of justice. (
"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100G?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

For non-conviction expungement, 100H allows a petitioner with not more than 2 records that do not include an adjudication or conviction to petition. The Commissioner has shorter notice/denial timelines than conviction expungement, and the DA may object. ("https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100H")
Time-based expungement eligibility checklist - 100I
Wilma should only route to time-based expungement if all of these are true:
Offense age All offenses occurred before the persons 21st birthday
Offense not excluded Offense is not listed in 100J
Waiting period - misdemeanor-only At least 3 years before petition, including incarceration, custody, or probation
Waiting period - any felony At least 7 years before petition, including incarceration, custody, or probation
Record count No other Massachusetts criminal/juvenile appearances or dispositions except minor motor-vehicle offenses and the records being expunged
Out-of-state/federal No other out-of-state, U.S. possession, or federal criminal/juvenile appearances or dispositions except minor motor-vehicle offenses
Active investigation Petitioner must certify they are not currently the subject of an active criminal investigation
Multiple offenses same incident Count as a single offense
These criteria come from 100I. (
"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100I")
Time-based expungement disqualifiers - 100J
Wilma should flag for attorney/manual review, and generally route away from time-based expungement, if the record involves:
death or serious bodily injury;
intent to cause death or serious bodily injury;
False ID / identity theft / court or law-enforcement error / fraud Non-time-based expungement 100K
Marijuana-only offense Marijuana expungement check Petition for Expungement of Marijuana Offenses; usually tied to 100K/decriminalized conduct logic

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100E")

pathways.label: Non-time-based expungement for false identity, error, fraud, or decriminalized conduct (§ 100K)
pathways.summary: Non-time-based expungement is governed by M.G.L. c. 276, 100K. Wilma should check this route when the record was created because of: Were you charged because someone used your name or identifying information? Unauthorized use or theft of identity Is the conduct now decriminalized or legalized?

ruleClauses: Non-time-based expungement is governed by M.G.L. c. 276, 100K.
Wilma should check this route when the record was created because of:
Were you charged because someone used your name or identifying information?
Unauthorized use or theft of identity
Is the conduct now decriminalized or legalized?
Was there a demonstrable police/prosecution error?
Civilian or expert witness error
Was the case based on a demonstrable witness or expert mistake?
Was the record created because of a clerical/court mistake?
Was there fraud that caused the record?

A court may order 100K expungement if it finds by clear and convincing evidence that the record was created because of one of those grounds. The court may also consider the best interests of justice, and it must enter written findings if it grants expungement. (
"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100K")
Wilma caution: This is not a general hardship expungement.

pathways.label: Marijuana-only expungement
pathways.summary: Massachusetts has a dedicated official form: Petition for Expungement of Marijuana Offenses Form. The official form asks the court to expunge marijuana-only charges, including possession of marijuana under G.L. c. 94C, 34. ("https://www.mass.gov/doc/petition-for-expungement-of-marijuana-offenses-form/download?utm_source=chatgpt.com") Wilma routing rule: Marijuana should not automatically mean eligible. Ask: 1 Was the record marijuana-only? 3 Was it possession, cultivation, possession with intent, distribution, or another marijuana offense? 4 Was the conduct decriminalized/legalized under current law?

sourceSections.title: How Wilma should route Massachusetts cases
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should start with the case outcome, not the offense name alone.

Users case outcome First Wilma route Main statute / form logic
Adult misdemeanor conviction Sealing Petition to Seal Conviction Records; generally 3-year wait
Adult felony conviction Sealing Petition to Seal Conviction Records; generally 7-year wait
Not guilty Sealing 100C; should be sealed unless defendant asks not to seal
No bill / grand jury no bill Sealing 100C; should be sealed
No probable cause Sealing 100C; should be sealed
Nolle prosequi Court sealing 100C; court must find substantial justice supports sealing
Dismissal Court sealing 100C; court must find substantial justice supports sealing
Juvenile delinquency / youthful offender record Juvenile sealing or time-based expungement check 100B for sealing; 100F/100I/100J for expungement
Adult conviction before age 21, limited record Time-based expungement check 100G, 100I, 100J
Adult non-conviction before age 21, limited record Time-based expungement check 100H, 100I, 100J
False ID / identity theft / court or law-enforcement error / fraud Non-time-based expungement 100K
Offense no longer a crime Sealing forthwith or non-time-based expungement 100A sealing; 100K expungement
Marijuana-only offense Marijuana expungement check Petition for Expungement of Marijuana Offenses; usually tied to 100K/decriminalized conduct logic
Massachusetts defines expungement as the permanent erasure or destruction of a record so it is no longer accessible to or maintained by the court, criminal justice agencies, or other government agencies. That is stronger than sealing. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100E")

sourceSections.title: Remedy definitions Wilma must understand
sourceSections.text:
Sealing
Sealing hides the record from most public/employer/housing background checks. It does not destroy the record. Criminal justice agencies, courts, and some authorized users may still access sealed information. Massachusetts law says sealed records generally do not disqualify a person from public service, and ordinary employment/housing/license applications must allow a person with a sealed record to answer
no record.
("https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/section100a")
Expungement
Expungement is permanent erasure/destruction. Massachusetts also says a person with an expunged record may answer
no record
for employment and licensing purposes, and expungement petition materials are confidential/nonpublic. ("https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100N?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma rule: Never tell a Massachusetts user
your record will be erased
unless the route is true expungement. For ordinary sealing, say
sealed from most public access,
not
deleted.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility - adult conviction sealing
sourceSections.text:
Adult conviction sealing is governed mainly by M.G.L. c. 276, 100A.
Basic adult sealing waits

Record type Waiting period Clock rule
Misdemeanor 3 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration or custody, must be at least 3 years before request
Felony 7 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration or custody, must be at least 7 years before request
Sex offense 15 years or longer 15 years after disposition/supervision/incarceration, or as long as registration duty continues, whichever is longer
Offense no longer a crime Immediate / forthwith Unless the same conduct remains criminal under a different designation
Section 100A requires no Massachusetts guilty finding during the applicable 3- or 7-year period, except minor motor-vehicle offenses punishable by a fine of not more than $50. It also requires the petitioner to state that they have no disqualifying out-of-state, federal, or U.S. possession convictions/imprisonment during the same lookback period. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/section100a")
Adult sealing disqualifiers / special handling

Issue Wilma treatment
Firearms convictions under M.G.L. c. 140 121-131H Generally not sealable under 100A
Public-corruption / obstruction-type convictions under c. 268 or c. 268A Generally not sealable under 100A, except resisting arrest
Sex offenses 15-year wait or registration-duty period, whichever is longer
Level 2 or Level 3 sex offender classification Not eligible to seal sex offenses
209A abuse-prevention order violation / 258E harassment-prevention order violation Treat as felony for sealing timing
Subsequent not guilty, dismissal, nolle prosequi, no bill Does not interrupt the waiting period
Old felony now classified as misdemeanor Treat as misdemeanor
Unknown felony/misdemeanor level Treat as misdemeanor
These rules come directly from 100As eligibility and exception language. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100A?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

sourceSections.title: Eligibility - non-conviction sealing
sourceSections.text:
Non-conviction sealing is governed mainly by M.G.L. c. 276, 100C.

Outcome Wilma route Standard
Not guilty Seal Commissioner/clerk/probation should seal, unless defendant asks not to
No bill Seal Commissioner/clerk/probation should seal
No probable cause Seal Commissioner/clerk/probation should seal
Nolle prosequi Court sealing petition Court must find that substantial justice would best be served
Dismissal Court sealing petition Court must find that substantial justice would best be served
Section 100C says not-guilty, no-bill, and no-probable-cause records are to be sealed by the Commissioner of Probation and the court/probation offices, unless the defendant makes a written request not to seal. Dismissals and nolle prosequi cases require a court finding that
substantial justice would best be served
by sealing. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100C")
Wilma caution: Do not treat every dismissal as automatically sealed. Dismissal/nolle cases require a court petition and a substantial-justice finding unless they qualify under another route.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility - juvenile sealing
sourceSections.text:
Juvenile sealing is primarily under M.G.L. c. 276, 100B.

Requirement Rule
Eligible record Delinquency court appearance record on file with Commissioner of Probation
Waiting period 3 years after court appearance/disposition, including supervision, probation, commitment, or parole, terminated
New Massachusetts record No adjudication delinquent or guilty finding in Massachusetts during preceding 3 years, except minor motor-vehicle offenses
Custody/commitment No imprisonment under sentence or juvenile commitment in Massachusetts during preceding 3 years
Out-of-state/federal No disqualifying adjudication, guilty finding, imprisonment, or commitment in another state, U.S. possession, or federal court during preceding 3 years
Section 100B gives the juvenile sealing criteria and requires the request to be made on a Commissioner-furnished form signed under penalties of perjury. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100B?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma caution: Juvenile sealing and juvenile/youthful-offender expungement are different. Sealing is broader. Expungement is narrower and requires the stricter 100I/100J checks.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility - time-based expungement
sourceSections.text:
Time-based expungement is available only in limited cases. It runs through 100F, 100G, 100H, 100I, and 100J.
Time-based expungement routes

Record type Statute Basic route
Juvenile delinquent / youthful offender adjudication 100F Petition Commissioner of Probation
Adult conviction 100G Petition Commissioner of Probation
Adult or juvenile record without conviction/adjudication 100H Petition Commissioner of Probation
For conviction expungement, 100G allows a petitioner with not more than 2 records of conviction to petition the Commissioner of Probation; multiple offenses from the same incident count as one offense for this purpose. The Commissioner certifies eligibility under 100I and 100J, the district attorney gets notice, and the court may grant or deny based on the best interests of justice. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100G?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
For non-conviction expungement, 100H allows a petitioner with not more than 2 records that do not include an adjudication or conviction to petition. The Commissioner has shorter notice/denial timelines than conviction expungement, and the DA may object. ("https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100H")
Time-based expungement eligibility checklist - 100I
Wilma should only route to time-based expungement if all of these are true:

Requirement Rule
Offense age All offenses occurred before the persons 21st birthday
Offense not excluded Offense is not listed in 100J
Waiting period - misdemeanor-only At least 3 years before petition, including incarceration, custody, or probation
Waiting period - any felony At least 7 years before petition, including incarceration, custody, or probation
Record count No other Massachusetts criminal/juvenile appearances or dispositions except minor motor-vehicle offenses and the records being expunged
Out-of-state/federal No other out-of-state, U.S. possession, or federal criminal/juvenile appearances or dispositions except minor motor-vehicle offenses
Active investigation Petitioner must certify they are not currently the subject of an active criminal investigation
Multiple offenses same incident Count as a single offense
These criteria come from 100I. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100I")
Time-based expungement disqualifiers - 100J
Wilma should flag for attorney/manual review, and generally route away from time-based expungement, if the record involves:
death or serious bodily injury;
intent to cause death or serious bodily injury;
dangerous weapon;
offense against an elderly or disabled person;
sex offense, child sex offense, or sexually violent offense;
OUI/OUI-type offense under c. 90 24;
firearms offenses under c. 140 121-131Q;
restraining-order / abuse-prevention / harassment-prevention order violations;
domestic assault and battery under c. 265 13M. ("https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100J?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma rule: Time-based expungement should be treated as a narrow
under age 21 + limited record + non-excluded offense
route. Most adult users will not qualify.

sourceSections.title: Eligibility - non-time-based expungement
sourceSections.text:
Non-time-based expungement is governed by M.G.L. c. 276, 100K.
Wilma should check this route when the record was created because of:

Ground Wilma prompt
False identification
Were you charged because someone used your name or identifying information?

Unauthorized use or theft of identity
Was this identity theft?

Offense no longer a crime
Is the conduct now decriminalized or legalized?

Law-enforcement error
Was there a demonstrable police/prosecution error?

Civilian or expert witness error
Was the case based on a demonstrable witness or expert mistake?

Court employee error
Was the record created because of a clerical/court mistake?

Fraud on the court
Was there fraud that caused the record?

A court may order 100K expungement if it finds by clear and convincing evidence that the record was created because of one of those grounds. The court may also consider the best interests of justice, and it must enter written findings if it grants expungement. (

"https://malegislature.gov/Laws/GeneralLaws/PartIV/TitleII/Chapter276/Section100K")
Wilma caution: This is not a general hardship expungement.
It is hurting my job search
supports sealing arguments, but it does not by itself create 100K expungement eligibility.

sourceSections.title: Marijuana-specific expungement
sourceSections.text:
Massachusetts has a dedicated official form: Petition for Expungement of Marijuana Offenses Form. The official form asks the court to expunge marijuana-only charges, including possession of marijuana under G.L. c. 94C, 34. ("https://www.mass.gov/doc/petition-for-expungement-of-marijuana-offenses-form/download?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma routing rule: Marijuana should not automatically mean eligible. Ask:
1 Was the record marijuana-only?
2 What was the exact charge?
3 Was it possession, cultivation, possession with intent, distribution, or another marijuana offense?
4 Was the conduct decriminalized/legalized under current law?
5 Was there another non-marijuana charge in the same case?
6 Which court handled the case?
If the user has marijuana-only conduct that appears decriminalized/legalized, route to the marijuana expungement form and manual verification against the docket.

sourceSections.title: Record request process
Massachusetts waiting-period table
sourceSections.text:
Route Wait Clock starts / runs from
Adult misdemeanor sealing 3 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration/custody
Adult felony sealing 7 years Court appearance/disposition, including incarceration/custody
Adult sex-offense sealing 15 years or registration-duty period, whichever is longer Disposition/supervision/probation/incarceration; level 2/3 sex offenses not sealable
Juvenile sealing 3 years Termination of court appearance/disposition, supervision, probation, commitment, or parole
Time-based expungement - misdemeanor-only 3 years Offense and any incarceration/custody/probation must be at least 3 years before petition
Time-based expungement - any felony 7 years Offense and any incarceration/custody/probation must be at least 7 years before petition
Non-time-based expungement No standard time wait Must prove false ID, identity theft, decriminalized offense, error, or fraud
Not guilty / no bill / no probable cause sealing No ordinary wait Should be sealed under 100C
Dismissal / nolle prosequi sealing No fixed statutory wait in 100C Court decides substantial justice

sourceSections.title: Wilma intake decision tree
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Identify record type
Ask:

Was this an adult criminal case, juvenile delinquency/youthful offender case, or are you not sure?

If adult go to Step 2.If juvenile/youthful offender check 100B sealing first, then time-based expungement if strict criteria may fit.If unknown ask for CORI/docket.
Step 2 - Identify outcome
Ask:

How did the case end: guilty/conviction, CWOF, dismissal, nolle prosequi, not guilty, no bill, no probable cause, or still pending?

If pending no sealing/expungement route yet; manual/legal review.If not guilty/no bill/no probable cause 100C sealing route.If dismissal/nolle 100C court sealing route.If conviction sealing first; expungement only if narrow criteria.
Step 3 - Check sealing
Ask:

Was it a misdemeanor or felony, and when did you finish jail, probation, parole, or any sentence?

Misdemeanor 3-year check.Felony 7-year check.Sex offense 15-year/registration/level check.Firearm/public-corruption exclusions manual review.
Step 4 - Check time-based expungement
Ask:

Were all the charges from before your 21st birthday, and do you have no more than two records total?

If no likely not time-based expungement.If yes check 100J exclusions, waiting period, no other records, no active investigation.
Step 5 - Check non-time-based expungement
Ask:

Was the record created because of identity theft, false identification, court/police/witness error, fraud, or because the conduct is no longer a crime?

If yes 100K review.If no route to sealing, not expungement.

Use safer language:

You may have a sealing route.

This looks like a possible expungement route, but the docket and offense statute need review.

Based on what you shared, this appears more likely to be sealing than expungement.

I cannot confirm eligibility until we know the disposition date, sentence completion date, offense statute, and whether there are pending/new charges.

Wilma should escalate/manual-review when:
sex offense or registration appears;
firearm offense appears;
domestic violence / 209A / 258E / restraining-order violation appears;
OUI appears;
offense involved serious bodily injury, death, dangerous weapon, elderly/disabled victim;
there are multiple cases across states;
user has pending charges;
user reports federal charges;
user is asking about immigration, licensing, housing authority, firearms rights, or professional licensing consequences;
docket disposition is CWOF, because Massachusetts users often misunderstand whether it counts as conviction/non-conviction for different purposes.

sourceSections.title: Agent-facing summary
sourceSections.text:
For Massachusetts, train Wilma like this:
Default route: sealing.Expungement route: narrow, mostly under-21 limited-record cases, marijuana/decriminalized conduct, false ID, identity theft, error, or fraud.Main adult sealing wait: 3 years misdemeanor, 7 years felony.Main non-conviction rule: not guilty/no bill/no probable cause should seal; dismissal/nolle requires substantial justice.Main danger: do not promise destruction unless expungement is actually available.
